pattern: The feasibility of robbing at least k non-adjacent houses without exceeding a given capability is monotonic in that capability, so binary search over the capability value with a greedy feasibility check finds the smallest one that works.
hints: Ask a simpler yes/no question first: for a fixed capability cap, what's the most non-adjacent houses you can rob while never touching a house worth more than cap?
That yes/no question is monotonic -- a bigger cap can only ever let you rob the same number of houses or more -- which is exactly what makes binary search over candidate cap values work.
Answering the yes/no question for a fixed cap is a single greedy left-to-right sweep: whenever the current house is within cap, rob it and skip the next one; otherwise move on.

edge_inputs.description: all houses hold the same amount
k at its maximum, forcing every other house to be robbed
k equal to one just needs the single cheapest house

stress.description: random house values with k scaled to a valid fraction of the house count
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, (n + 1) // 2)
    return [nums, k]
